text: This video will demonstrate the important concepts, key steps and fundamental anatomical considerations in laparoscopic ventral mesh rectopexy in a female patient who has previously undergone mesh hysteropexy. This case is of an otherwise healthy, forty-eight year old female who has previously undergone sacrohysteropexy for uterine prolapse. She presents with mixed symptoms of obstructed defecation and urge fecal incontinence. Defecating proctography, anorectal manometry, ultrasound, and examination under anesthesia were used to investigate her symptoms. At the time of preoperative proctography, the patient could not evacuate the contrast agent from the rectum. This was associated with an excessive perineal descent. Examination under anaesthesia was subsequently performed to exclude an internal rectal prolapse. A circular anal dilator device was used to assess for an internal rectal prolapse, either into the level of an existing rectocele or onto or beyond the anal sphincter and canal. These select pictures are an example of the findings from this patient's EUA. which demonstrated a grade IV internal rectoanal interception, the highest grade of internal rectal prolapse according to the Oxford prolapse grading system. The pathological descent of the rectal wall into the anal canal that defines a grade IV prolapse is demonstrated diagrammatically here.

The surgical field is accessed using four trocars, with an additional port on the left side due to anticipated difficulties from previous hysteropexy. The patient is positioned in a steep Trendelenburg to enhance pelvic exposure. The uterus is fixed to the anterior abdominal wall, and the sigmoid colon is secured to the anterolateral abdominal wall. The sacral promontory is clearly visualized with the peritoneum tented by mesh from the previous hysteropexy. The right ureter is visible lateral to the right common iliac artery. The pouch of Douglas is well exposed without active traction, and a large right ovarian cyst is present, posing a technical challenge.

This diagrammatic representation demonstrates how the uterus becomes suspended by its attachment to the promontory. The general principle in sacro-hystereopexy involves circumferentially securing mesh around the cervix and then extending a connected arm of mesh toward the sacral promontory where it is secured. The peritoneum is subsequently closed over the mesh.

The peritoneum is visibly tented by the mesh extending from the cervix to the sacral promontory, with a previous tack visible through the peritoneum. The surgeon begins dissection by incising the peritoneum to expose the sacral promontory, releasing the mesh from prior hysteropexy to facilitate access to the lateral aspect of the rectum. Dissection proceeds medially into the rectovaginal space and continues down to the pelvic floor, preserving autonomic nerves in the posterior rectal space. Digital examination aids in dissection down to the rectopelvic junction. The mesh is placed into the peritoneum and secured to the anterior rectal wall using PDS suture material through five millimeter cannulas, ensuring superficial bites include the seromuscular layers while leaving the mucosa intact.

The surgeon uses a surgesis biological mesh, placing four sutures along the lateral aspects of the rectum and one suture medially to secure the mesh circumferentially. The mesh is then pulled towards the sacral promontory, restoring the rectum to its original anatomical position while carefully avoiding extraneous tension that could alter the anorectal angle.

The surgeon incises and dissects the peritoneum overlying the sacral promontory. A tack is initially placed into the peritoneum, causing bleeding due to lack of tensile strength, and is subsequently removed. The promontory is palpated, and a new tack is inserted into the presacral fascia. Mesh from a previous hysteropexy is released and then sutured together to preserve the original repair, ensuring it is securely attached to the presacral fascia.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum over the underlying mesh to prevent adhesion to the small bowel and surrounding structures, completing the ventral rectopexy procedure.
